notes: The U-M discovery field names Hue de Rotelande as author, but direct source and modern scholarship identify Hue as author of the Anglo-Norman source and the three English adapters as anonymous.

entities.description: The unique manuscript witness of Ipomadon A, text-bearing on current ff. 191r–335r in a 15th-century paper miscellany. Kölbing's old foliation gives ff. 188a–332a and records a lost leaf after old f. 234.

physicalDescription.extent: 377 currently foliated leaves plus covers, endleaves, and flyleaves represented in the 768-image sequence

entities.description: The unique manuscript witness used for Ipomydon B, on ff. 54r–84r, in the first of two late-medieval literary booklets later compiled with other matter by John Colyns.
entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 2252

notes: The Ipomydon booklet occupies ff. 54–85; the work itself is ff. 54r–84r. The wider composite's 1460–1540 range is not a composition date for B.

dateStatements.label: reported printing
dateStatements.notes: The date and STC identity are later bibliographic identifications; Kölbing's directly observed relevant extent is one leaf.

entities.description: A bibliographic representation of the early printed fragment Kölbing called W and collated against B. Kölbing explicitly says only one leaf among three associated Bagford leaves belongs to Ipomydon; the other two belong to another romance. Later identification as STC 5732.5 and the exact current shelfmark require current-catalog confirmation.
entities.label: Wynkyn de Worde Ipomydon fragment W
notes: This entity is not modeled as the source of B; it is a later printed witness used by Kölbing for variant readings.

reviewNotes: The cataloging subject is Kölbing's edition-level compilation; the three anonymous English adaptations, their manuscripts, Hue's Anglo-Norman source, the U-M encoding, and the repository XML remain distinct layers.

Complete-object claims are provider-relative and explicit: the BSB bundle covers all 676 canvases; Chetham covers the complete 768-image exposed page sequence but has no spine or edge views; Harley and Longleat have no obtainable complete image object at review time.
No complete Chetham cache was created under restrictive terms. The locally cached Meale plates are partial witness evidence and are not substitutes for complete manuscript facsimiles.

No reusable source-evaluation or modeling problem was found beyond the skill's existing rules on catalog boundaries, provider-relative completeness, restricted viewer extraction, current-access negatives, and separation of textual, physical, editorial, and digital layers.

lineage.summary: CME00025 is the repository XML for a University of Michigan manually keyed and proofread electronic text derived from selected matter in Eugen Kölbing's 1889 Ipomedon in drei englischen Bearbeitungen. The encoding contains the edition title page and printed pp. 3–358: three independently derived anonymous Middle English adaptations, A and B in verse and C in incomplete prose, plus local German apparatus. It omits the dedication, preface, CLXXXI-page introduction, pp. 359–484 notes and reference matter, blanks, and binding. Kölbing edited A from Chetham's MS 8009 (Mun.A.6.31), B from British Library Harley MS 2252 with readings from one relevant leaf of an early de Worde fragment, and C from Longleat House MS 257, formerly Bath MS 25. A complete 676-canvas BSB scan of the 1889 physical volume is cached. Chetham's complete 768-image public LUNA inventory was verified but deliberately not bulk-cached because current Manchester terms prohibit systematic extraction of substantial parts. Current complete images are unavailable for Harley MS 2252, and no exact public catalog or complete digital object was found for Longleat MS 257; selected manuscript plates in Meale's 1984 article are explicitly partial.

catalogSummary.author: Three anonymous Middle English adapters
catalogSummary.date: A: late 14th–mid-15th century; B: second half of the 15th century; C: 15th century; edition published 1889
catalogSummary.editor: Eugen Kölbing; Joseph Hall collated Ipomadon A against its manuscript
catalogSummary.region: England; A is localized broadly to the North Midlands and B to the Northeast Midlands, while C's compositional locality remains unresolved
catalogSummary.title: Ipomedon in drei englischen Bearbeitungen

catalogingSubject.label: Ipomedon in drei englischen Bearbeitungen
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The edition-level compilation represented by University of Michigan CME item CME00025: Eugen Kölbing's 1889 presentation of three independently derived anonymous Middle English adaptations of Hue de Rotelande's Anglo-Norman Ipomedon, conventionally called A, B, and C, together with encoded German title matter and local editorial apparatus. This record does not collapse the three adaptations, Hue's source romance, the three physical manuscripts, Kölbing's complete printed volume, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML into one abstract medieval work.

contentParts.label: Encoded edition title matter and local apparatus

locators.value: main title page; half-titles pp. 255 and 321; local apparatus pp. 3–358
title matter, headings, 2,059 NOTE, 357 PB, and 717 MILESTONE elements

contentParts.summary: The German edition title page, part titles and half-titles, headings, page and manuscript milestones, and interleaved textual notes retained from Kölbing; the long introduction and back matter are omitted.

contentParts.label: Ipomadon A

locators.value: 191r–335r; f. 335v is blank in the digital sequence

notes: The encoded 8,890 line elements preserve Kölbing's edition lineation. They are not a newly normalized critical count.
contentParts.summary: The long stanzaic tail-rhyme adaptation, edited from Chetham's Library MS 8009 (Mun.A.6.31).

contentParts.label: The Lyfe of Ipomydon B
contentParts.summary: The shorter rhyming-couplet adaptation, edited from British Library Harley MS 2252 with variants from the surviving de Worde fragment Kölbing called W.

contentParts.label: Ipomedon C

locators.value: 11 DIV2 units; 192 P; 76 NOTE; ending marked by an ellipsis

notes: The ending is textually incomplete because a manuscript leaf is missing; it is not merely omitted by the U-M encoding.
contentParts.summary: The incomplete prose adaptation from Longleat House MS 257 (formerly Bath MS 25); physical losses include ff. 101 and 106, the latter containing the ending.

dateStatements.display: Kölbing's edition-level compilation was published in 1889
notes: This is the publication date of the cataloging subject, not the composition date of any medieval adaptation.

dateStatements.display: Ipomadon A was composed between the late 14th and mid-15th century
Ipomydon B was composed in the second half of the 15th century
Ipomedon C is a 15th-century prose adaptation
notes: The Longleat manuscript's proposed 1457–1469 production window is witness evidence, not a narrower composition date for the adaptation.

extent.description: Three edited adaptations in six XML DIV1 units: A has 8,890 L elements in 751 LG groups, B has 2,346 L elements in 9 LG groups, and C has 11 DIV2 units containing 192 P elements. The whole XML also contains 2,059 NOTE, 357 PB, and 717 MILESTONE elements. These are encoding metrics, not normalized canonical counts.

formStatements.description: Two verse adaptations and one prose adaptation, with prose German title matter and editorial apparatus

notes: Hue de Rotelande is modeled as author of the related Anglo-Norman source, not as author of the three anonymous Middle English adaptations, despite the inherited U-M discovery field.
The record distinguishes medieval composition, witness production, 1889 editorial compilation, U-M encoding, and repository artifact dates.

The A and B line totals, stanza-group totals, C paragraph counts, notes, page breaks, and milestones are representation metrics from the XML and Kölbing's lineation, not newly normalized critical extents.
Longleat's proposed York production context and Harley's London booklet context belong to physical-witness lineage; neither is transferred automatically into work-level composition geography.
The complete Chetham LUNA inventory was verified but not bulk-cached because current Manchester terms prohibit systematic extraction or reutilization of substantial parts.

next_steps: Ask Chetham's Library or the Database of Middle English Romance to reconcile the explicit blank-folio image label with the published terminal locator; retain f. 335r as the observed text-bearing end meanwhile.
openQuestions.question: Should Ipomadon A's current terminal locus be cited as f. 335r, where the text visibly ends, or as f. 335v, as stated by the Database of Middle English Romance?

next_steps: Monitor the exact British Library record for restored images and enquire with Longleat about MS 257's microfilm-PDF eligibility, physical coverage, cost, and reuse terms.
openQuestions.question: Can complete, reusable digital objects be obtained for Harley MS 2252 and Longleat House MS 257?

next_steps: Confirm the modern STC identity and shelfmark in a current British Library or ESTC record before treating later three-leaf descriptions as equivalent to Kölbing's explicitly one-leaf Ipomydon fragment.
openQuestions.question: What exact current catalog record, shelfmark, surviving extent, and digital availability correspond to Kölbing's de Worde fragment W?

notes: This broad cultural origin does not transfer any surviving witness's production place to the lost authorial text.
Breslau is not asserted as a medieval place of composition.
The three Middle English texts are independently derived adaptations; anonymous responsibility is therefore modeled separately for each part.

scope.description: Manuscript-based texts and apparatus in the 1889 edition

notes: Kölbing's preface says Hall read the proofs with the Chetham manuscript beside him and supplied corrections; this role does not extend to B or C.

scope.description: Proofreading and manuscript collation of Ipomadon A

summaries.value: Three independent Middle English adaptations of Hue de Rotelande's Anglo-Norman Ipomedon. A is an 8,890-line tail-rhyme romance; B is a shorter 2,346-line couplet romance; C is an incomplete prose rendition in eleven encoded divisions. Across differing forms, the versions narrate Ipomedon's concealed identity, courtship of the heiress of Calabria, hunting, tournaments, military tests, separation, and recognition. Kölbing presents the texts separately with manuscript-based readings and extensive German apparatus.

CME00025 encodes the edition title page and printed pp. 3–358: the three edited Middle English texts and their local apparatus. It omits the dedication, preface, CLXXXI-page introduction, pp. 359–464 endnotes, corrections, indexes, abbreviations, blanks, and binding matter of the complete 1889 object.

titles.value: Ipomedon in drei englischen Bearbeitungen. Herausgegeben von Eugen Kölbing

work.author: Three anonymous Middle English adapters
work.dateDisplay: A: late 14th–mid-15th century; B: second half of the 15th century; C: 15th century; edition published 1889
work.editor: Eugen Kölbing; Joseph Hall collated Ipomadon A against its manuscript
work.regionDisplay: England; A is localized broadly to the North Midlands and B to the Northeast Midlands, while C's compositional locality remains unresolved
work.summary: Three independent Middle English adaptations of Hue de Rotelande's Anglo-Norman Ipomedon. A is an 8,890-line tail-rhyme romance; B is a shorter 2,346-line couplet romance; C is an incomplete prose rendition in eleven encoded divisions. Across differing forms, the versions narrate Ipomedon's concealed identity, courtship of the heiress of Calabria, hunting, tournaments, military tests, separation, and recognition. Kölbing presents the texts separately with manuscript-based readings and extensive German apparatus.
